perturbation.original_text: Neither Recipient will, within twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement, directly or indirectly solicit the employment or consulting services of or employ or engage as a consultant any of the officers of Provider or any other employees or contractors of the Provider engaged in evaluating the Potential Transaction or information about whom has been provided to Recipient in connection with evaluating the Potential Transaction, so long as they are employed by the Provider and for six months after they cease to be employed by Provider.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Recipient will, within eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, directly or indirectly solicit the employment or consulting services of or employ or engage as a consultant any of the officers of Provider or any other employees or contractors of the Provider engaged in evaluating the Potential Transaction or information about whom has been provided to Recipient in connection with evaluating the Potential Transaction, so long as they are employed by the Provider and for six months after they cease to be employed by Provider.
perturbation.explanation: The change extends the non-solicitation period to eighteen months, which might be deemed unreasonably long under California law, potentially restricting employee mobility without a strong business justification. California favors employee freedom to change jobs.
perturbation.contradicted_law: CA Business and Professions Code § 16600 - Contracts restraining profession, trade or business void
perturbation.law_explanation: CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrowly tailored non-solicitation clauses may be enforceable, an 18-month restriction, especially after employment ends, is more likely to be viewed as an unlawful restraint of trade, particularly if it's not necessary to protect trade secrets or other legitimate business interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from 12 to 18 months. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest this could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, specifically CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing BPC 16600, states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The extended duration increases the likelihood that the clause would be considered an unlawful restraint, as it more severely limits employee mobility. The longer duration without a strong business justification is likely to fail legal scrutiny in California.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall terminate five years after the date of this Agreement (except with respect to trade secrets for which the term shall be perpetual).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate one year after the date of this Agreement (except with respect to trade secrets for which the term shall be perpetual).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the agreement's term to one year, excluding trade secrets, might not allow sufficient time to protect confidential information that does not rise to the level of a trade secret, potentially conflicting with common law principles of unfair competition. Some confidential information needs more than a year to retain its competitive value.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 41 - Appropriation of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Restatement itself is not a statute, it reflects common law principles of unfair competition recognized in many states, including Delaware. By limiting the agreement to only one year, the contract may inadequately protect confidential information that, while not meeting the strict definition of a trade secret, still deserves protection under unfair competition law. Section 41 addresses the improper appropriation of trade secrets, but related sections cover other confidential information which may require longer protection periods than one year. A one year protection might be insufficient depending on the industry/information.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the agreement's term from five years to one year. The Law Explanation suggests this may inadequately protect confidential information that, while not meeting the strict definition of a trade secret, still deserves protection under unfair competition law, potentially violating common law principles. While the scraped snippets do not directly address term lengths, the Law Explanation, referencing section 41 and related sections on confidential information, argues that one year may be insufficient depending on the industry/information. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this contradicts principles of unfair competition, as some confidential information needs more than a year to retain its competitive value. Therefore, the reduced term creates a potential contradiction.
